text:
Tin-based catalysts have been widely reported to have a high potential for selective CO2 reduction into hydrocarbons via electrochemical reduction of CO2 (CO2RR). However, the structure of active sites and the identification of reaction intermediates are unknown to date. In this paper, a series of single-Sn-atom catalysts with controlled coordination environments were designed and prepared in situ. The pre-treatment of carbon nanotubes with functional species facilitated the assembly of Sn-phthalocyanine (SPc) molecules, which led to the creation of the desired catalyst. By controlling the species and the amount of functional species, the catalyst comprising of near-100% axially oxygen-coordinated Sn(IV) species (O-Sn-N4) exhibits a high catalytic performance and selectivity to formic acid.

The surface tin species active for CO2 hydrogenation was investigated using operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy, attenuated total reflectance surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and 119Sn Mössbauer spectroscopy. After thorough washing, in situ measurements on the active tin species supported on amorphous alumina revealed unambiguously the formation of surface bidentate tin carbonate species composed of tin center in a relatively low oxidation state along with the information of the dynamic evolution of the tin electronic and coordination states during CO2 hydrogenation process. The properties of different active sites and their binding characteristics with *OCHO during catalysis were modeled using density functional theory. By exploring different possible local structures at the active sites with an O-Sn-N4 composition, our calculation suggested a high likelihood of forming Sn-O-CO2 species that in turn favors binding *OCHO through different interaction routes, and thereby significantly reduces the activation energy for hydrogenation of *OCHO toward the selectivity toward formic acid product. The mechanistic role of p-block single-atom metal centers during CO2 hydrogenation has been elucidated in this study.
